coredata.dc:title: Boosting photoanodic activity for water splitting in carbon dots aqueous solution without any traditional supporting electrolyte
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hong-Yan
Hu, Rong
Wang, Na
Hu, Gui-Lin
Wang, Kai
Xie, Wei-Hua
Cao, Rui
coredata.dc:description: We present a PEC water oxidation system with boosting activity in Carbon Dots (CDs) aqueous solution directly without any traditional supporting electrolyte. For α-Fe2O3 photoanode, photocurrent was significantly enhanced by dozens of folds compared to that in Na2SO4 solution under similar conditions. Based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, energy alignment measurements, photovoltage decay and Mott-Schottky plot, mechanism for charge transfer and function of CDs was well characterized. As semiconductors, CDs can combine with α-Fe2O3 into heterojunction for charge separation. As electrolyte, it can induce downward shift in conduction band of α-Fe2O3, allowing for stronger driving force for charge migration. Since CDs solution possesses a negative Zeta potential, it is proposed that during PEC proceeding, static interaction between CDs and positively charged photoanodic substrate can induce a dynamic balance of CDs absorbing or desorbing on/off α-Fe2O3 surface, which accounts for fast charge transfer, boosted photocurrent and high stability in PEC water splitting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEC water oxidation
Carbon dots
Accelerated charge transfer
Development in electrolyte
Heterojunction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of a-Fe2O3 modified FTO electrode
Characterization of material on electrode surface
Measurements for PEC activity and gas detection to evidence PEC performance
Formation and general properties of materials

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Representation for CDs where “blue ball” stands for the nanocrystalline sp2 carbon core characterized based on literature [14]; b) Zeta potential of CDs aqueous solution.

Four cases: (1) α-Fe2O3 in 45 mg mL−1 CDs aqueous solution; (2) α-Fe2O3 in 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution; (3) CDs&α-Fe2O3 in 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution; (4) CDs&α-Fe2O3 in 45 mg mL−1 CDs aqueous suspension. a) LSV of each case in the dark or under irradiation of 400 nm LED respectively; b) chopped light irradiation curve of each case with irradiation of 400 nm LED.

Three cases: (1) α-Fe2O3 in 45 mg mL−1 CDs aqueous solution; (2) α-Fe2O3 in 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution; (3) CDs&α-Fe2O3 in 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. a) IPCE and b) ABPE of each case respectively.

Three cases: (1) α-Fe2O3 in 45 mg mL−1 CDs aqueous suspension; (2) α-Fe2O3 in 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution; (3) CDs&α-Fe2O3 in 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. a) EIS of each case in the dark or under irradiation of 400 nm LED respectively; b) OCVD plots of α-Fe2O3 in 45 mg mL−1 CDs in the dark or under irradiation of 400 nm LED lamp from the electrolyte-electrode side (ΔV is the difference in voltage between dark and illumination conditions).

a) Mott-Schottky plots of three cases: (1) α-Fe2O3 in 45 mg mL−1 CDs; (2) α-Fe2O3 in 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution; (3) CDs&α-Fe2O3 in 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution respectively; b) The determined energy levels for explanation to the enhancement of PEC with α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution.
The proposed mechanism for PEC water oxidation on α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution.
CVs of a) α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution; b) α-Fe2O3 in Na2SO4 solution; c) CDs&α-Fe2O3 in Na2SO4 solution containing 1 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]with different scan rate under irradiation.

head.ce:title: Boosting photoanodic activity for water splitting in carbon dots aqueous solution without any traditional supporting electrolyte

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Macromolecular Science of Shaanxi Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi’an, 710119, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Macromolecular Science of Shaanxi Province
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Scientific Research and Academic Office, Air Force Logistics University, Xuzhou, 221000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Applied Surface and Colloid Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi’an, 710119, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Applied Surface and Colloid Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We present a PEC water oxidation system with boosting activity in Carbon Dots (CDs) aqueous solution directly without any traditional supporting electrolyte. For α-Fe2O3 photoanode, photocurrent was significantly enhanced by dozens of folds compared to that in Na2SO4 solution under similar conditions. Based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, energy alignment measurements, photovoltage decay and Mott-Schottky plot, mechanism for charge transfer and function of CDs was well characterized. As semiconductors, CDs can combine with α-Fe2O3 into heterojunction for charge separation. As electrolyte, it can induce downward shift in conduction band of α-Fe2O3, allowing for stronger driving force for charge migration. Since CDs solution possesses a negative Zeta potential, it is proposed that during PEC proceeding, static interaction between CDs and positively charged photoanodic substrate can induce a dynamic balance of CDs absorbing or desorbing on/off α-Fe2O3 surface, which accounts for fast charge transfer, boosted photocurrent and high stability in PEC water splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: Water oxidation occurring on the photoanode attracts intense interests, because its enhancement in photoelectrochemical (PEC) activity can significantly trigger the efficient electron consumption to produce hydrogen in opposite direction [1]. Due to its harvesting absorption can span over the wide solar spectrum, pristine α-Fe2O3, as a typical prototype semiconductor, can endow the excited hole migration for water oxidation under visible-light irradiation [2,3]. Besides, α-Fe2O3 also possesses several impressive advantages including chemical stability, environmental benign and abundance, and appears at the forefront as a tempting research target at present [3]. Nevertheless, notoriously short hole diffusion length in α-Fe2O3 always induces poor charge transport and slow interfacial kinetics, which suppresses its ability to perform efficiently [4]. Therefore, via heteroatom doping [5], nanostructuring [6], constructing heterojunctions [7] or assembling molecular and semiconductor hybrid [8] to fabricate photoanode, has been identified as the most favorable strategies to conquer the challenges. Alternatively, to enhance the competency of photoanode, exploring the proper and novel electrolyte seems much more convenient and effective [9]. This is because the interaction of electrolyte and metal oxide interface can tune the conduction band (CB) edge position of photoelectrode, which plays a pivotal role in determining the charge injection and quality of photocurrents [10]. However, until now, little attention is paid to how to modulate electrolyte for tailoring the PEC activity.

Carbon dots (CDs), typically surface-functionalized by COOH, COOR, and OH moieties etc., can be highly dispersed as colloidal in water [11]. Assisted by COOH, CDs can be assembled on photoanodic semiconductors surface via dip-coating [12]. Additionally, with suitable band alignment, electronic coupling between π states of CDs and energy band of photoanode can potentially fabricate into heterojunctions [11]. Coupled with the ability of CDs to degrade H2O2 chemically, it is essential to integrate for efficient PEC water oxidation [13]. However, owing to weak interaction of assembled components, long-period photoelectrolysis always renders CDs ruthlessly to detach from photoelectrode surface, accompanied by a sharp decrease in photocurrent [12a]. Auspiciously, colloidal nature of CDs enables to build an electronic layer surrounding the surface associated with PEC process, which can induce a specific movement of CDs in the electronic field. Therefore, CDs aqueous solution can be potentially exploited as the electrolyte directly. In this end, here, we practice PEC water oxidation of α-Fe2O3 photoanode directly in a negatively-charged CDs colloidal solution rather than anchor CDs on α-Fe2O3 surface to assemble into an integrated photoanode. Typically, photocurrent of α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution significantly achieves dozens of times for that in traditional electrolyte, and duration for PEC water oxidation was also prolonged. Mechanism study evidences that such CDs solution can impart its versatility in n-type photoanode activated PEC water oxidation system. This alternation launches a new application of materials and represents that a small change initiates a big revolution. It provides a more striking and facile strategy to tailor the activity in a durable and robust pathway, which may inspire a new thinking for fabricating PEC system.

CDs were prepared based on the literature via hydrothermal treatment [14]. Sodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA·2Na) (2 g, 0.054 mol) was applied as a precursor, which was dissolved into 20 mL deionized water and then kept in autoclave for 10 h at 200°C. The resultant solution was cooled down and then dialyzed with Slide-A-Lyzer G2 Dialysis Cassettes (2 K MWCO) for 12 h. Dialysis was repeated for several times until conductivity of surrounding distilled water was not significantly changed. After a series of operation for the residue including centrifugation, washing with deionized water and freeze-drying, CDs was obtained as a light-yellow powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of a-Fe2O3 modified FTO electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Modification of FTO electrode with α-Fe2O3 for the preparing photoanode was based on our published method without further development [8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of material on electrode surface
ce:section.ce:para: Using a FEI Quanta 200 field emission scanning electron microscope (FEI-SEM), morphologies of electrode surface were examined, which gave images with an acceleration voltage of 5 or 10 kV. In order to ensure their chemical homogeneity, the materials were analyzed at several local spots. Energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) was conducted on an AMETEK Materials Analysis EDX which was equipped on scanning electron microscopy (SEM). To obtain clearer microstructure, transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20) as well as high resolution TEM (HRTEM) was applied. NMR data (1H NMR and 13C NMR) was recorded with 400 MHz Bruker. Chemical shifts are expressed in parts per million (ppm) units downfield from TMS. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the electrodeposited film was performed on a Kratos AXIS ULTRA XPS instrument. The C 1s peak at 284.8 eV was referenced for correcting the binding energy. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was performed on Malvern Instrument (England). The light intensity (Pλ) at each wavelength (λ) was determined using the FZ-A photometer with the input optical power of 8.37 mW cm−2. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopies (EIS) were carried out in a frequency range from 1000 kHz to 0.1 Hz at 5 mV amplitude of perturbation. To determine the pH of solution, pH meter Orion star A211 from ThermoFisher company was used. In addition, electro-conductibility was measured by conductibility meter DDS-11A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements for PEC activity and gas detection to evidence PEC performance

ce:section.ce:para: Unless otherwise noted, all electrochemical experiments were operated at 25°C on a CH Instruments electrochemical analyzer (model CHI660E). A three-electrode system was equipped with Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) as reference electrode, graphite rod as a counter electrode, and 3 cm2 FTO electrode covered by 2 cm2 α-Fe2O3 (or CDs&α-Fe2O3) as a working electrode. All potentials were reported versus RHE without further statement. All the photocurrent density is determined after dividing photocurrent by photoanode areas.

For detection of generated H2O2, rotating ring (Pt)-disk (GC) electrode (RRDE) was used. Such the electrochemical measurements were conducted in Ar-saturated 45 mg mL−1 CDs suspension at pH 7. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) of disk electrode was operated with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1, accompanied by the electrolysis of ring electrode at 1.61 V.

Hydrogen and oxygen was detected on a Shimadzu GC-14B instrument, whose amount was determined by injection Methane as an internal standard in the well-sealed PEC system. For detecting the H2O2 degradation properties of CDs, standard Clark-type oxygraph electrode (Hansatech Instruments) was applied, which was separated from the sampled solution by a Teflon membrane. For all experiments, the cell was thermostated at 20°C. The signal was calibrated using air saturated aqueous solutions ([O2] = 276 μM, T = 20°C), and then recorded for the entire duration of the measurement using Oxygraph + software (Hansatech Instruments) at 0.1 s intervals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation and general properties of materials
ce:section.ce:para: CDs is prepared with sodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA·2Na) as precursors via hydrothermal treatment for 10 h at 200°C [14]. Based on literature, it has been well characterized to consist of a nanocrystalline sp2 carbon core and carbonyl, carboxylate, and amine groups on the surface (Fig. 1 a), which was evidenced by the combination of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and NMR analysis (Figs. S1 and S2). After careful purification and then freeze-drying, CDs was obtained as a light-yellow powder, which can afford a bright-yellow colloidal upon dispersing into water. In dynamic light scattering (DLS), CDs sample gave a concentrated scattering intensity with hydrated particle size centered at 21 nm (Fig. S3). A typical absorption around 300−400 nm was assigned to π→π* conjugate structure shown in Fig. S4, which can expend the wide absorption to visible light range [16]. In combination with excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) in Fig. S5, it suggests that CDs can be activated by visible-light irradiation [17]. Zeta potential was further determined with the value of −48.2 mV (Fig. 1), which implies the negatively-charged CDs can move to the hole-cumulated photoanode by electrostatic interaction during PEC proceeding.

Pristine α-Fe2O3 modified FTO electrode was prepared based on our published method [8]. Under irradiation using LEDs (λ = 400 nm), PEC activity of photoanode in CDs aqueous solution (named as α-Fe2O3 in CDs) is tested assisted by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) (all potentials reported herein are referenced to RHE). Photocurrent density is measured under constant illumination conditions. Considering the assembled-ability of CDs on semiconductor, a reference photoanode CDs&α-Fe2O3 was prepared with bare α-Fe2O3 electrode directly immersing into 90 mg mL−1 CDs aqueous solution for 5 h. As shown in Fig. 2 , in 0.1 M pH 7.0 Na2SO4 solution, pristine α-Fe2O3 photoanode can generate a photocurrent density of 27 μA cm−2 at 1.23 V and CDs&α-Fe2O3 presents a slight enhancement in electronic response (32 μA cm−2) as expected. To be surprised, α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution can achieve a robust activity, where the concentration of 45 mg mL−1 CDs aqueous solution was selected as the best condition (Fig. S6). Typically, the photocurrent sharply jumped after 0.4 V, which further sustains over the entire potential range with the value reaching up to 0.64 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V. This value is almost 24 times of that in Na2SO4 solution with the same α-Fe2O3 substrate. Direct comparison of efficiency with other reports based on α-Fe2O3 substrate is difficult due to the different conditions used for material preparation or activity testing in each lab. However, as far as we know, the enhancement in activity to operate PEC water splitting in CDs solution is much more remarkable compared to the traditional strategies such as heteroatom doping, nanostructuring or assembling molecular and semiconductor hybrid, and so on [5–8]. Noted that all the testing conditions lead to negligible dark currents, photo-response current accordingly stem from the light-excited charge-separation and migration [18]. Owing to the most photoactive performance originated from α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution, it implies that the system can fascinate the most facile charge transport, which can efficiently suppress the charge recombination [19]. Moreover, compared with α-Fe2O3 in Na2SO4 displaying open-circuit voltages at 0.6 V, CDs aqueous solution can initiate the cathodic shift to 0.4 V, which demonstrates the migration of holes at interface of photoelectrode and electrolyte can be more easily succeeded in [20]. Additionally, the performance of CDs&α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution was further detected in Fig. 2, which exhibits a slightly stronger photocurrent at the beginning compared to that when pristine α-Fe2O3 applied, but the difference is smaller accompanied with the increase of applied potential. Such slight enhancement in photocurrent at the beginning might be originated from CDs dip-coating on the surface of α-Fe2O3, while the less difference implies that the CDs can ruthlessly drop off from α-Fe2O3 surface [12a] during PEC proceeding.

As the precursor of CDs, it is observed that EDTA·2Na can also trigger the enhanced photocurrent as depicted in Fig. S7, which has been reported as sacrificial reagents for photo-induced water oxidation [21]. In 0.1 M EDTA·2Na aqueous solution, after 2 h controlled-potential electrolysis under continued visible-light irradiation, α-Fe2O3 film almost totally fall off from the substrate, but it can still keep integrated after working in CDs solution under the same condition (Fig. S8a). Carefully checking the UV–vis absorption of EDTA·2Na solution after photoelectrolysis, a representative signal assigned to Fe3+ coordinated with EDTA was observed, implying EDTA solution can bring out the serious corrosion of α-Fe2O3 electrode (Fig. S8b). This is consistent with the report that boosted photocurrent in the presence of EDTA is originated from oxidation and decomposition of EDTA rather than water [22]. Opposite to the above phenomenon, when CDs solution was applied, the intensity of UV–vis absorption of electrolyte can be well sustained after 2 h photoelectrolysis, and α-Fe2O3 film can also firmly anchor on the substrate (Fig. S8c). Consequently, different from the sacrificial reagents role of EDTA, CDs is extremely stable during the PEC process, which can induce robust activation for water oxidation and also photoelectrode from serious corrosion.

Furthermore, transient photocurrent on LSV curves of α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution presents the monotonically increasing with the potential along with the light on or off (Fig. 2b). Under each illumination, it shows a prompted rise in photocurrent, and quickly return to the baseline once the light is switched off, demonstrating charge transport is quite fast [23]. Transient photocurrent responses versus time with chopped light irradiation brings out the reproducible photocurrent with almost identical value for each on/off cycles, highlighting the satisfied stability for PEC performance (Fig. S9). Additionally, under continuous illumination, the wavelength-dependent incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra were recorded by chronoamperometry with the bias of 1.23 V. As shown in Fig. 3 a, α-Fe2O3 in CDs colloidal yields the most prominent IPCE under each wavelength measurement, which is identical with the observation in LSV results. The magnified IPCE plots in the incident wavelength between 380 and 530 nm exhibit in the inset in Fig. 3. In Na2SO4 solutions, α-Fe2O3 showed no obvious changes in IPCE with the integrated photocurrent density, while IPCE promotion was observed for α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution and CDs&α-Fe2O3 respectively in the visible region, which evidences that CDs can act as semiconductors, and generate electron-hole pairs under light irradiation [24]. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) displays the similar amount of CDs can attach on α-Fe2O3 substrate after photoelectrolyzing α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution or directly soaking α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution (CDs&α-Fe2O3) with the same period (Fig. S10), implying besides of semiconducting properties, much more improved PEC performance of α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution should be attributed to some other dominant reasons. Fig. 3b further points out the applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) in each typical case. In CDs solution, α-Fe2O3 reaches the maximum conversion efficiency of 1.09 % at 0.81 V vs. RHE, further suggesting the most efficient separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Additionally, prolonging the photoelectrolysis was operated, which shows the photocurrent of α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution can be sustained without distinct decay after 10 h, demonstrating a robust stability during PEC water oxidation (Fig. S11). Before and after photoelectrolysis of α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution, pH of the system was slightly changed from 7.22 to 7.13. This implies the system can split water into H2 and O2 simultaneously.

We further carried out the PEC activity of α-Fe2O3 photoanode in NaOH aqueous solution with the similar pH (pH around 7.3) to CDs solution (Fig. S12a), which showed photocurrent around 10 μA cm−2 at 1.5 V (vs. RHE), much smaller than that in CDs solution. Considering the ionic strength decreasing in NaOH aqueous solution, a solution of 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution with NaOH at pH 7.3 was further applied. The photocurrent is increased, but only reaching up to 40 μA at 0.9 V, which is still much smaller than that of α-Fe2O3 photoanode in CDs solution (Fig. S12b). Usually, electro-conductibility is a typical representative for ionic strength in the electrolyte. The electro-conductibility of NaOH aqueous solution at pH 7.3, 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution with NaOH at pH 7.3, 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution as well as 45 mg mL−1 CDs aqueous suspension was detected respectively. As shown in the Table S1, 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution holds the highest electro-conductibility, but affords a very low water oxidation photocurrent. Therefore, it is not either the ionic strength or pH of the system that enhances the activity of α-Fe2O3 photoanode in CDs solution, but some other main cofactor should be seriously taken into account.

To illustrate the origin of PEC activity, we performed electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) response with Nyquist plots, as illustrated in Fig. 4 a. The measurement can reflect the electronic resistance for charge transfer across the interface between electrode and electrolyte [25]. In accordance with the highest PEC photocurrent response, employing irradiation with α-Fe2O3 soaked in CDs solution, can yield the smallest arc radius. This means that in such system, kinetic barrier for charge migration at the surface of electrode is dramatically ameliorated, which evidenced that CDs solution can fascinate the fastest charge transport and the longest charge carriers service lifetime [25]. As another available approach, open circuit voltage decay (OCVD) was identified for the trap-state mediated electron-hole separation in semiconductor materials [18,26]. The trap state conditions can be extracted from charge decay lifetime and the photovoltage (ΔV) based on photovoltage-time (V-t) spectra in Figs. 4b and S13 respectively. According to Eq. S10−11, the average charge decay lifetime of each V-t profiles are fitted by a biexponential function, which affords the harmonic mean of lifetime τm and total half life as log (2 × τm) [27]. In CDs solution, τm is determined as 10.4 s and total half life is 1.32 s on α-Fe2O3 surface. Soaking α-Fe2O3 or CDs&α-Fe2O3 in Na2SO4 solution can provide τm of 26.5 or 25.3 s, and total half life of 1.72 s or 1.70 s respectively. Clearly, charge decay on α-Fe2O3 surface in CDs solution is much faster than those on the surface of α-Fe2O3 or CDs&α-Fe2O3 in Na2SO4 solution. These results signify CDs solution can dramatically deactivate the surface trap, which efficiently suppresses serious charge recombination on electrode surface [28]. Thus, more electrons are driven towards the cathode for hydrogen production, leading to evident improvement in PEC efficiency for splitting water. Moreover, photovoltage (ΔV) can provide more information about charge separation. The α-Fe2O3 photoanode in CDs solution shows a ΔV value of 0.21 V, much higher than those presented by α-Fe2O3 or CDs&α-Fe2O3 in Na2SO4 solution (0.087 V and 0.083 V respectively). Usually higher value of ΔV corresponds to better efficiency for photo-charge separation [29]. Clearly, this is in line with the best charge separation in the system with α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution. Additionally, the OCVD measurement of CDs&α-Fe2O3 was also carried out in CDs solution, which affords τm of 12.4 s, total half life of 1.39 s as well as ΔV of 0.18 V (Fig. S13). These values are very close to those of α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution. It is implied that beside the semiconductor properties of CDs on the α-Fe2O3 surface, CDs solution plays a more crucial role for the enhancement of PEC water oxidation.

In order to determine the functions of CDs in PEC system, a series of measurements were carried out. Initially, the interaction between α-Fe2O3 and CDs was observed by the electronic properties of systems. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, in accordance with the typical of n-type semiconductor, the Mott-Schottky plots exhibit positive slopes under irradiation, which means that the nature of α-Fe2O3 substrate can be sustained in three sampled conditions. Based on the Mott-Schottky equation (Eq. S12), the carrier densities Nd of electrodes are estimated [30], with the values for α-Fe2O3 in CDs or Na2SO4 solution and assembled CDs&α-Fe2O3 photoanode of 1.36 × 1021 cm−3, 1.09 × 1021 cm−3 and 1.14 × 1021 cm−3, respectively. Read from Mott-Schottky plots, flat band potential Vfb was determined as 0.50 V and 0.48 V with bare α-Fe2O3 and CDs&α-Fe2O3 in Na2SO4 solution respectively, while CDs solution endows α-Fe2O3 to exhibit a much more positive value of 0.68 V. As a negligible gap difference can be assumed between Vfb potential and bottom edge of CB for n-type semiconductor [31], compared with Na2SO4 solution, it is evident that CDs solution can entail a downward shift in CB energy of α-Fe2O3 film. Clearly, CDs assembled to form CDs&α-Fe2O3 can not seriously affect the energy band location of α-Fe2O3 film, but CDs in solution can induce the dramatic band shift in α-Fe2O3 film. The reason for downshift in CB is on-going now, which may be originated from the interaction of electrolyte and metal oxide interface to modulate the film charge and thus the energetics [32].

Based on above analysis, CDs can also perform as semiconductor in the system. To validate the semiconductor effect of CDs on PEC activity, the aligned band structures for the combination of CDs and α-Fe2O3 was determined. As our previous determination from UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum, pristine α-Fe2O3 photoanode holds the energy gap (Eg) of α-Fe2O3 for 2.07 eV [8]. Owing to that CDs solution entails a shift for Vfb, the CB band of α-Fe2O3 is evaluated to 0.68 V instead, leading to the VB level moved to 2.75 eV. According to CV measurement of oxidation and reduction potentials (Eox and Ered, respectively), the HOMO and LUMO levels of CDs were recorded at 1.57 eV and −0.33 eV, respectively (Fig. S14) [33]. Accordingly, both VB and CB of α-Fe2O3 locate at more positive potential values compared with HOMO and LUMO band of CDs respectively. Therefore, it is confirmed that CDs and α-Fe2O3 can construct a heterojunction for charge migration in CDs solution. A schematic diagram depicted in Fig. 5b clearly pinpoints the thermodynamical favour for electron-hole pair migration on the basis of band alignment for α-Fe2O3 and CDs. As expected, under irradiation, the electrons on HOMO of CDs and VB of α-Fe2O3 can be both excited to the individual LUMO or CB level, respectively. Due to deeper energy level, the electrons from LUMO band of CDs can quickly transfer to the CB of α-Fe2O3, and then migrates for hydrogen production. On the other hand, holes can move from VB of α-Fe2O3 to HOMO of CDs, leading to the efficient charge separation. Owing to that CDs solution can entrust α-Fe2O3 with much lower location of CB and VB levels, it can manifest a much more powerful driving force ΔG for charge injection. Gerischer theory announced that the logarithm of electron transfer rate is a quadratic function with respect to driving force ΔG [34]. Therefore, it signifies that the enlarged driving force can accelerate charge migration, followed by a significant improvement for photocurrent response.

It is reported that CDs can also act as efficient hole scavengers to trigger the degradation of H2O2 chemically. In order to monitor the intermediate for photoelectrolyzing α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution, a series of operation was carried out. Assisted by rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE), the electron transfer number for water oxidation of α-Fe2O3 in 45 mg mL−1 CDs solution is in the range of 2.11 and 3.02, suggesting that the reaction pathway is dominated by a 2-electron transfer process from H2O to H2O2, with a 45 % yield for H2O2 production (Fig. S15) [35]. Additionally, during photoelectrolysis of α-Fe2O3 in the absence of CDs, H2O2 was detected by the addition of potassium iodide (Fig. S16), but it disappeared in the presence of CDs (Fig. S17) [36]. In combination with the observation that large amounts of H2 and O2 were detected and quantified by gas chromatography (GC) after photoelectrolyzing α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution (Fig. S18), it is implied that H2O2 is an intermediate product, which is generated from water oxidation by the holes left on the VB of α-Fe2O3 and further decomposed by CDs into O2 (Fig. 5b). Meanwhile, in this case, holes are efficiently consumed to oxidize water rather than α-Fe2O3 photoanode, thus stabilizing α-Fe2O3 against photo-corrosion [37].

In order to enclose why CDs solution can induce the efficient PEC activity of α-Fe2O3, in combination with the negative Zeta potential of CDs solution, a mechanism was proposed. As a typical n-type semiconductor, photo-generated electron can be repelled from the surface to the bulk of the solid, where they diffuse through the external circuit to counter electrode for hydrogen production. Thus, holes can be left and accumulate on photoanode surface for driving water oxidation [38]. As a result, a positively-charged surface of photoelectrode can generate an extrinsic electric field, stabilized by the components in the surrounding electrolyte [10]. Along this line, on α-Fe2O3 surface, a positively-charged internal electronic field can be built, which attracts negatively-charged CDs colloidal to be approached, followed by the faster mass transfer [39]. After attracting holes, CDs are repulsed into solution for further production of O2. Finally, a dynamic balance of CDs absorbing or desorbing on/off the surface of α-Fe2O3 substrates is proposed. In combination with the semiconductor properties of CDs, it is assumed that a “dynamic heterojunction” for charge migration in CDs solution can be constructed (Fig. 6 ). This proposal is supported by several facts: 1) CDs assembled into CDs&α-Fe2O3 can not seriously affect the performance for the α-Fe2O3 photoanode, but CDs solution dominantly accounts for the enhancement in photocurrent; 2) transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Fig. S19) manifests that compared to soaking α-Fe2O3 into CDs solution directly, photoelectrolyzing α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution can be much easier to anchor CDs on α-Fe2O3 uniformly. This might imply that owing to a much more uniformly positively-charged field constructed on the surface of α-Fe2O3 during PEC proceeding, attracting negative CDs to be approached is much easier; 3) we further explored the PEC activity test to other n-type or p-type semiconductors. As shown in Figs. S20−21, typical n-type WO3 [40] and TiO2 [41] indicates the much higher photoelectronic response in CDs solution than soaking them into Na2SO4 solution, similar to the happenings for α-Fe2O3. However, employing a photocathodic material such as Cu2O [42] instead, CDs solution initiates a negligible effect on PEC activity upon irradiation, while whose photocurrent is visibly enhanced in Na2SO4 solution (Fig. S22). Clearly, the negatively charged CDs solution can accelerate the charge transport for the n-type semiconductor photoanodes, but suppress the activity of a p-type photocathodic material. All together, it evidences that the interaction between α-Fe2O3 and CDs solution can induce faster charge transfer, leading to the efficient PEC water oxidation. In this case, fast charge consumption is driven, giving birth to a significant enhancement for PEC water splitting. More detail in mechanism study is being dug into now.

The faster photo-charge transfer on α-Fe2O3 surface in CDs solution was further probed by ferrocyanide/ferricyanide couple ([Fe(CN)6]4−/3−) [43]. In the dark, the cyclic voltammetry (CVs) exhibits the similar redox currents of [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− on anodic surface under three sampled conditions in Fig. S23. Analyzed by Laviron’s method, the redox kinetics ks of [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− were all around 0.13 s−1 [44]. This means in the dark, almost no differences in charge migration were observed in three cases. Moreover, under irradiation (Fig. 7 ), α-Fe2O3 and CDs&α-Fe2O3 in Na2SO4 solution maintained low current intensity for [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− redox couples, corresponding to low electron transport rate. However, in a sharp contrast, α-Fe2O3 in CDs solution exerts a more pronounced photocurrent, accompanied by a slight changes in Ep,a and Ep,c with increasing scan rate. This emphasizes that the photo-charge transfer on α-Fe2O3 surface in CDs solution is very fast, although it is difficult to evaluate Ks value directly under the conditions.

In summary, we report an efficient PEC water oxidation system with pristine α-Fe2O3 as photoanode in a negatively charged CDs solution under visible-light irradiation. Here, CDs performs both semiconductor and electrolyte with several functions in PEC water oxidation: 1) it can built a heterojunction with α-Fe2O3 based on the proper energy alignment, allowing for an efficient charge separation; 2) as a electrolyte, it enables a downward shift in CB band of α-Fe2O3, leading to the enlarged driving force for charge transfer; 3) it functions as efficient hole scavengers to trigger the degradation of water oxidation intermediate H2O2; 4) the interaction between negatively-charged CDs solution and α-Fe2O3 photoanode surface during PEC proceeding is proposed to build a dynamic balance of CDs absorbing or desorbing on/off the α-Fe2O3 substrate surface. All together, these advantages signify an accelerated charge transfer, accompanied by the distinct increase in photocurrent, PEC efficiency as well as the stability of the system. Here, we focus on the new application of materials. As the non-parallel example to execute PEC water splitting in CDs aqueous suspension directly in the absence of any other traditional supporting electrolyte, it represents a more striking and facile strategy to tailor the activity in a durable and robust pathway. More research about exploring other CDs solution with specific photoelectrode for PEC water splitting is also no-going now.

Hong-Yan Wang: Visualization, Formal analysis, Writing-review & editing, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Rong Hu: Visualization, Data curation, Formal analysis. Na Wang: Visualization, Data curation. Gui-Lin Hu: Visualization, Data curation. Kai Wang: Writing-review & correcting. Wei-Hua Xie: Writing-review & correcting. Rui Cao: Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing-review & editing, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.
The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22072082), National Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (2019JQ-175), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (GK201903041, GK202103026).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2021.120378.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
